one_line_takeaway: REACT trains few-shot MGT detectors against a co-evolving, RAG-guided black-box humanization attacker plus a sample-efficient pairwise contrastive loss, lifting average F1 by 4.95 points and cutting average attack success rate by 3.66pp versus 8 SOTA detectors across 4 datasets and 4 shot sizes.
key_finding: Overall average across all 4 datasets/4 shots/3 seeds: REACT achieves 93.60 accuracy / 93.72 F1, beating the best metric-based detector (Binoculars) by +7.66 accuracy / +8.70 F1, and beating the strongest model-based baseline by +5.19 accuracy / +4.95 F1. REACT attains 10.04% average ASR under the 4 attacks, a 3.66-percentage-point reduction versus the best baseline per dataset-shot setting (largest gain of 6.47pp at 32-shot), and lowers max ASR by 8.20pp on average (up to 23.81pp on RAID at 128-shot). REACT has the lowest average ASR in 14/16 dataset-shot settings and lowest max ASR in 15/16. It also shows markedly lower variance across seeds than adversarially trained baseline RADAR (e.g., DetectRL 32-shot: REACT 99.46+/-0.58 vs RADAR 87.76+/-12.49 accuracy; OUTFOX 32-shot: REACT 95.64+/-1.56 vs RADAR 77.34+/-16.01). On the hardest benchmark, RAID, REACT's accuracy rises from 79.30+/-0.90 (32-shot) to 92.43+/-1.52 (256-shot), a +13.13-point gain, and at 64-shot outperforms GREATER (best adversarially trained baseline) by +7.34 points and CoCo (best few-shot-tailored baseline) by +6.31 points. The ablation shows RAG with a single retrieved pair beats both Direct Prompt and multi-shot Few-shot Prompting baselines on the accuracy-ASR trade-off while using less context/training cost; few-shot prompting is also less stable across shot sizes (e.g., 4-shot prompting ASR swings from ~20% at 128-shot to ~70% at 64-shot on RAID).
core_contribution: The paper proposes REACT (RAG-GuidEd Attacker Strengthens ConTrastive Few-shot Detector), an adversarial training framework for few-shot machine-generated text (MGT) detection. REACT couples (1) an attacker that uses retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to craft highly humanized adversarial rewrites of MGT under a black-box, output-label-only threat model, retrieving a 'most human-like' and 'most machine-like' exemplar pair from a self-expanding retrieval pool to condition an LLM generator, and (2) a target detector trained with a combined Adversarial Classification Loss (label correctness on clean + adversarial samples, with an alpha-downweighted adversarial term) and a novel Pairwise Boundary Contrastive (PBC) loss that pulls same-label [CLS] representations together and pushes different-label ones apart on a single (X, rewrite) pair rather than requiring large in-batch negative sets. The attacker's surrogate detector and the target detector are updated alternately (co-evolution) at each training step, with the surrogate aligned to the target via pseudo-labels obtained from single-query black-box feedback.
threat_model: Output-only black-box setting following Biggio & Roli (2018)-style threat modeling: the attacker has no access to the target detector's weights, architecture, or proprietary training data beyond the shared few-shot set, and can only observe top-1 predicted labels via one query per generated rewrite (including auxiliary rewrites created during training). The attacker may use any public LM/LLM (open- or closed-source) plus previously generated rewrites and their returned labels to build stronger future adversarial examples via the retrieval pool. The detector, conversely, has access only to the few-shot labeled set, attacker-produced adversarial examples, and HWT-based auxiliary rewrites; it does not query LLMs itself at deployment. Note per the paper's own framing: this is explicitly a defensive threat model (the attacker module exists only to harden the detector during training), and the authors state in their Ethics Statement that they withhold an end-to-end evasion pipeline and systematically optimized prompts to limit misuse.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluated only on English benchmarks -- multilingual extension is untested; (2) adversarial training increases training time and computational/resource overhead versus non-adversarial baselines; (3) the detector operates at the document level and cannot localize machine-generated spans within human-AI co-authored text, so sentence/token-level attribution is left to future work. Additional grounding-based observations: the black-box threat model assumes the attacker gets exactly one label-only query per rewrite -- robustness against attackers with richer query budgets, confidence scores, or true zero-query transfer attacks is not evaluated in the main text portion reviewed here. The paper is authored by 3 researchers at a single institution (Xi'an Jiaotong University) with no stated external replication.

quantitative_results: Overall average accuracy 93.60 / F1 93.72 for REACT
+7.66 accuracy / +8.70 F1 over best metric-based detector (Binoculars)
+5.19 accuracy / +4.95 F1 over strongest model-based baseline
Average ASR under 4 attacks: 10.04%, a 3.66pp reduction vs best per-setting baseline
Average ASR reduction of 6.47pp at 32-shot specifically
Max ASR reduced by 8.20pp on average; up to 23.81pp on RAID at 128-shot and 18.77pp on OUTFOX at 256-shot
Lowest average ASR in 14/16 dataset-shot settings; lowest max ASR in 15/16
RAID 64-shot: REACT 84.55+/-3.78 vs GREATER +7.34pp, vs CoCo +6.31pp
RAID 128-shot: REACT surpasses GREATER by +3.70 accuracy points
